question_text: A heating element has a resistance of 24 Ω. When connected to a 12 V power supply, calculate the current flowing through the heating element. Then, if the voltage is increased to 18 V (keeping the same resistance), calculate the new current and explain what happens to the power dissipated by the heating element.
correct_answer: At 12 V: I = V/R = 12/24 = 0.5 A. At 18 V: I = V/R = 18/24 = 0.75 A. The current increases by 0.25 A (50% increase). The power increases from P = VI = 12 × 0.5 = 6 W to P = 18 × 0.75 = 13.5 W, more than doubling.
explanation: Using Ohm's Law (I = V/R), we find the currents at each voltage. When voltage increases by 50%, current also increases proportionally. Power (P = VI) increases even more dramatically because it depends on both voltage and current, so the heating effect is much stronger at higher voltage.
